Inarisha.jpg|Inari Shrine Kawasukune-jinja(Kawajima) Shinmyousha.jpg|Shinmei Shrine Kawasukune-jinja(Kawajima,Shibukawa) Hokushinsha.jpg|Hokushin Shrine === Cultural Properties === === Shibukawa City Designated Important Tangible Cultural Property === * Kawasukune Shrine {{jalink|Sangaku|Q2569069}} (designated January 30, Showa 50){{Cite web |title=Designated Cultural Properties of Former Shibukawa City Area |website=www.city.shibukawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-08-20 |language=ja}} – Measuring 190 cm in width and 80 cm in height, it was dedicated in {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}} 3 ({{jalink|1856|Q7676}}) by Chiaki Keietsu, a native of Tsukiji Village, {{jalink|Tone District|Q1077001}} (present-day {{jalink|Katashina Village|Q1203661}}). The sangaku calculates the base length of a right triangle using algebra{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1986|pp=273-274}}. === Shibukawa City Designated Important Intangible Cultural Property === * Kawashima {{jalink|Lion Dance|Q713866}} (designated October 20, Showa 47) – A ritual performed by the Kawashima Lion Dance Preservation Society to pray for warding off evil, good health, and abundant harvests. It is dedicated at the annual festival of the subordinate Suwa Shrine (early October). Though believed to have been interrupted by the eruption of Mount Asama in Tenmei 3 (1783), an extant storage box for the lion heads bears the date {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} 4 ({{jalink|1833|Q7587}}), suggesting it was revived around that time. The dance is performed by a single dancer wearing a waist drum, and the lion heads consist of three types: male lion (Hōgan), female lion, and child lion{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1986|pp=299-230}}. === Access === * 15-minute walk from {{jalink|Kanashima Station (Gunma Prefecture)|Q6360686|Kanashima Station}} on the {{jalink|Agatsuma Line|Q391568}} of {{jalink|East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|JR East}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book|ja |title=Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Gazetteer |date=1925-10-20 |year=1925 |publisher=Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Education Association |ref=harv |author=Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Education Association |doi=10.11501/1020918}} * {{Cite book|ja |title=Stone Monuments and Cultural Properties |date=1986-3-31 |year=1986 |publisher=Shibukawa City |ref=harv |editor=Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee |doi=10.11501/12424075}}{{Registration required}} * {{Cite book|ja |title=History of Shibukawa City |date=1993-3-31 |year=1993 |publisher=Shibukawa City |ref=harv |editor=Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee |volume=Vol. 2: General History (Ancient–Early Modern) |doi=10.11501/9644681}}{{Registration required}} * {{Cite book|ja |title=History of Shibukawa City |date=1984-10-1 |year=1984 |publisher=Shibukawa City |ref=harv |editor=Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee |volume=Vol. 4: Folklore |doi=10.11501/9643284}}{{Registration required}} === Related Articles === * {{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Kōzuke Province|Q11360308}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kawasukune Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:群馬県の神社]] [[Category:群馬県の旧郷社]] [[Category:上野国の式内社]] [[Category:渋川市の建築物]] [[Category:渋川市の歴史]] [[Category:群馬県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:区市町村指定無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:need translation]]

Worship hall |Location = {{jalink|Gunma Prefecture|Q129499}}{{jalink|Shibukawa City|Q851492}} Kawashima 1287 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deities = {{Ublist| {{jalink|Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}} | {{jalink|Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|Hayakitsuhime no Kami}} }} |Sacred Object = {{jalink|Agatsuma River|Q1636255}} |Shrine Rank = {{Ublist| {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor) candidate shrine | {{jalink|Kōzuke Province|Q1054838}}{{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379|Fourth Shrine}} | Former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} }} |Founded = {{jalink|Hōki|Q18520676}} 2 ({{jalink|771|Q29950}}) September 29 |Chief Priest = |Main Hall Style = {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Other Name = Sukune Daimyōjin |Pilgrimage Status = |Annual Festival = Sunday closest to {{jalink|October 9|Q2923}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Gunma|label=Kawasu
kune Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kawasukune Shrine''' (かわすくねじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Kawashima, {{jalink|Shibukawa City|Q851492}}, {{jalink|Gunma Prefecture|Q129499}}. The enshrined deities are {{jalink|Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}} and {{jalink|Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|Hayakitsuhime no Kami}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|p=543}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha||minor shrine listed in the Engishiki}} of {{jalink|Kōzuke Province|Q1054838}}, {{jalink|Gunma District|Q5617131}}. Its former shrine rank was {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}. === History === It is said to have been founded on September 29, {{jalink|Hōki|Q18520676}} 2 ({{jalink|771|Q29950}}){{Sfn|Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Education Association|1925|pp=575-577}}. According to the shrine records, the Kawasukune Shrine in Hakoshima Village (present-day {{jalink|Higashiagatsuma Town|Q1203419}} Hakoshima), the Sukune Shrine in Ubajima Village (present-day Ubajima, Shibukawa City; now {{jalink|Takeuchi Shrine (Shibukawa City)|Q66085129|Takeuchi Shrine}}), and the Kawasukune Shrine in Yuage Village (present-day Miyukida, Shibukawa City) were all divided and enshrined from this shrine during the {{jalink|Bunan|Q1193586}} era ({{jalink|1444|Q6420}}–{{jalink|1449|Q6446}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=883-884,933-934}}. Interpreting “Kōha” as “river” and “Sukune” as “straight root,” the shrine name is understood to mean “the main stream of the river.” Because three shrines stood along the {{jalink|Agatsuma River|Q1636255}} in places named Hakoshima, Ubajima, and Kawashima—all containing the word “island”—{{jalink|Kizao Ozaki|Q21698487}} explains that the river itself was deified{{Cite book|ja |title=Research on the Jinmyōchō of Kōzuke Province |date=1974-12-10 |year=1974 |publisher=Ozaki-sensei Book Publication Society |pages=210-212 |last=Ozaki |first=Kizao |author-link=Kizao Ozaki |doi=10.11501/12266236}}{{Registration required}}. In July of {{jalink|Tenmei|Q1194121}} 3 ({{jalink|1783|Q7745}}), the shrine buildings were washed away by mudflows caused by the {{jalink|Tenmei Great Eruption|Q106959236|eruption of Mount Asama}}, but they were rebuilt at the present location on September 19, Tenmei 5 ({{jalink|1785|Q7756}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=235}}. The main hall dates to the Tenmei era, and the worship hall is believed—based on a {{jalink|munafuda|Q8008834}}—to have been built in {{jalink|Kōka|Q1325317}} 2 ({{jalink|1845|Q7636}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=236}}. During the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, a Shugendō temple called Kōmyō-ji existed as the shrine’s {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755}}, but it was abolished after the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=237,960-962}}. Kanmae Shrine manuscript){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=233-234}} * {{jalink|Kansei|Q1195131}} 5 (1794) August 26 – Senior First Rank (Kawasukune Daimyōjin) ({{jalink|Sōgen senji}}){{Sfn|Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Education Association|1925|pp=575-577}} * Meiji 13 (1881) May – GōshaMeiji Shrine Records{{Sfn|Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Education Association|1925|pp=575-577}} * Meiji 42 (1909) February 27 – Shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements{{Sfn|Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Education Association|1925|pp=575-577}} === Enshrined Deities === '''Primary Deities''' * {{jalink|Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}}, {{jalink|Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|Hayakitsuhime no Kami}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|p=543}} '''Jointly Enshrined''' * Ōyamatsumi no Kami * Hondawake no Kami :: Deities formerly enshrined at the unranked Hachiman Shrine in Nishiozuka, Kawashima Village. Although a “Shrine Preservation Petition” submitted by parishioners in Meiji 11 was approved and spared them from consolidation, they were eventually consolidated in July Meiji 40{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=876}}. '''Additionally Enshrined''' * {{jalink|Ōmononushi|Q10936701|Ōmononushi no Kami}}, {{jalink|Ukanomitama|Q3080728|Ukanomitama no Kami}}, {{jalink|Mizuhanome|Q8191796|Mizuhanome no Kami}} Kawashima-kawasukune-jinja Main hall.jpg|Main hall Kawashima-kawasukune-jinja Main hall inside.jpg|Main hall decorations === Subordinate Shrines === '''Sessha''' * Suwa Shrine : (Primary deities) {{jalink|Takeminakata|Q9150391|Takeminakata no Kami}}, {{jalink|Yasakatome|Q9150392|Yasakatome no Kami}} :: Due to mudflows from the eruption of Mount Asama in Tenmei 3, it was relocated together with Kawasukune Shrine from Aza Butai to the current site. The “Kawashima Lion Dance” is a ritual of Suwa Shrine{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=875-876}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|pp=639-643}}. * Shinmei Shrine : (Primary deity) {{jalink|Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|Amaterasu Ōmikami}} :: The former “Ise Shrine” enshrined at Tenjinhara was relocated and renamed Shinmei Shrine{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=876-877}}. * Inari Shrine – (Primary deity) {{jalink|Ukanomitama|Q3080728|Ukanomitama no Kami}} * Hokushin Shrine – (Primary deity) {{jalink|Ame-no-Minakanushi|Q2842779|Ame-no-Minakanushi no Kami}} '''Massha''' * {{jalink|Ame-no-Minakanushi|Q2842779}}, {{jalink|Kamimusubi||Kamimusubi no Kami}}, {{jalink|Takamimusubi||Takamimusubi no Kami}}, Amaterasu Ōmikami * Tenjin Shrine * {{jalink|Sarutahiko|Q3090037|Sarutahiko Ōkami}} * Kōshin Shrine * Kuraikami – consolidated on June 18, {{jalink|Kansei|Q1195131}} 7 ({{jalink|1796|Q7818}}) * Gozu Tennō – consolidated on June 15, {{jalink|Kansei|Q1195131}} 7 ({{jalink|1796|Q7818}}) * {{jalink|Kenrō Jishin|Q11427926}} – consolidated December {{jalink|Kyōwa|Q960763}} 3 ({{jalink|1804|Q6894}}) * {{jalink|Daikokuten|Q1759916}} Kawasukune-jinja(Kawagima,Shibukawa) Suwa-jinja.jpg|Suwa Shrine Kawasukune-jinja(Kawajima,Shibukawa) Inarisha.jpg|Inari Shrine Kawasukune-jinja(Kawajima) Shinmyousha.jpg|Shinmei Shrine Kawasukune-jinja(Kawajima,Shibukawa) Hokushinsha.jpg|Hokushin Shrine === Cultural Properties === === Shibukawa City Designated Important Tangible Cultural Property === * Kawasukune Shrine {{jalink|Sangaku|Q2569069}} (designated January 30, Showa 50){{Cite web |title=Designated Cultural Properties of Former Shibukawa City Area |website=www.city.shibukawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-08-20 |language=ja}} – Measuring 190 cm in width and 80 cm in height, it was dedicated in {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}} 3 ({{jalink|1856|Q7676}}) by Chiaki Keietsu, a native of Tsukiji Village, {{jalink|Tone District|Q1077001}} (present-day {{jalink|Katashina Village|Q1203661}}). The sangaku calculates the base length of a right triangle using algebra{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1986|pp=273-274}}. === Shibukawa City Designated Important Intangible Cultural Property === * Kawashima {{jalink|Lion Dance|Q713866}} (designated October 20, Showa 47) – A ritual performed by the Kawashima Lion Dance Preservation Society to pray for warding off evil, good health, and abundant harvests. It is dedicated at the annual festival of the subordinate Suwa Shrine (early October). Though believed to have been interrupted by the eruption of Mount Asama in Tenmei 3 (1783), an extant storage box for the lion heads bears the date {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} 4 ({{jalink|1833|Q7587}}), suggesting it was revived around that time. The dance is performed by a single dancer wearing a waist drum, and the lion heads consist of three types: male lion (Hōgan), female lion, and child lion{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1986|pp=299-230}}. === Access === * 15-minute walk from {{jalink|Kanashima Station (Gunma Prefecture)|Q6360686|Kanashima Station}} on the {{jalink|Agatsuma Line|Q391568}} of {{jalink|East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|JR East}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book|ja |title=Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Gazetteer |date=1925-10-20 |year=1925 |publisher=Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Education Association |ref=harv |author=Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Education Association |doi=10.11501/1020918}} * {{Cite book|ja |title=Stone Monuments and Cultural Properties |date=1986-3-31 |year=1986 |publisher=Shibukawa City |ref=harv |editor=Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee |doi=10.11501/12424075}}{{Registration required}} * {{Cite book|ja |title=History of Shibukawa City |date=1993-3-31 |year=1993 |publisher=Shibukawa City |ref=harv |editor=Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee |volume=Vol. 2: General History (Ancient–Early Modern) |doi=10.11501/9644681}}{{Registration required}} * {{Cite book|ja |title=History of Shibukawa City |date=1984-10-1 |year=1984 |publisher=Shibukawa City |ref=harv |editor=Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee |volume=Vol. 4: Folklore |doi=10.11501/9643284}}{{Registration required}} === Related Articles === * {{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Kōzuke Province|Q11360308}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kawasukune Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:群馬県の神社]] [[Category:群馬県の旧郷社]] [[Category:上野国の式内社]] [[Category:渋川市の建築物]] [[Category:渋川市の歴史]] [[Category:群馬県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:区市町村指定無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:pages]]

According to the shrine records, the Kawasukune Shrine in Hakoshima Village (present-day {{jalink|Higashiagatsuma Town|Q1203419}} Hakoshima), the Sukune Shrine in Ubajima Village (present-day Ubajima, Shibukawa City; now {{jalink|Takeuchi Shrine (Shibukawa City)|Q66085129|Takeuchi Shrine}}), and the Kawasukune Shrine in Yuage Village (present-day Miyukida, Shibukawa City) were all divided and enshrined from this shrine during the {{jalink|Bunan|Q1193586}} era ({{jalink|1444|Q6420}}–{{jalink|1449|Q6446}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=883-884,933-934}}. Interpreting “Kōha” as “river” and “Sukune” as “straight root,” the shrine name is understood to mean “the main stream of the river.” Because three shrines stood along the {{jalink|Agatsuma River|Q1636255}} in places named Hakoshima, Ubajima, and Kawashima—all containing the word “island”—{{jalink|Kizao Ozaki|Q21698487}} explains that the river itself was deified{{Cite book|ja |title=Research on the Jinmyōchō of Kōzuke Province |date=1974-12-10 |year=1974 |publisher=Ozaki-sensei Book Publication Society |pages=210-212 |last=Ozaki |first=Kizao |author-link=Kizao Ozaki |doi=10.11501/12266236}}{{Registration required}}. In July of {{jalink|Tenmei|Q1194121}} 3 ({{jalink|1783|Q7745}}), the shrine buildings were washed away by mudflows caused by the {{jalink|Tenmei Great Eruption|Q106959236|eruption of Mount Asama}}, but they were rebuilt at the present location on September 19, Tenmei 5 ({{jalink|1785|Q7756}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=235}}. The main hall dates to the Tenmei era, and the worship hall is believed—based on a {{jalink|munafuda|Q8008834}}—to have been built in {{jalink|Kōka|Q1325317}} 2 ({{jalink|1845|Q7636}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=236}}. During the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, a Shugendō temple called Kōmyō-ji existed as the shrine’s {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755}}, but it was abolished after the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=237,960-962}}. Association|1925|pp=575-577}} === Enshrined Deities === '''Primary Deities''' * {{jalink|Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}}, {{jalink|Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|Hayakitsuhime no Kami}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|p=543}} '''Jointly Enshrined''' * Ōyamatsumi no Kami * Hondawake no Kami :: Deities formerly enshrined at the unranked Hachiman Shrine in Nishiozuka, Kawashima Village. Although a “Shrine Preservation Petition” submitted by parishioners in Meiji 11 was approved and spared them from consolidation, they were eventually consolidated in July Meiji 40{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=876}}. '''Additionally Enshrined''' * {{jalink|Ōmononushi|Q10936701|Ōmononushi no Kami}}, {{jalink|Ukanomitama|Q3080728|Ukanomitama no Kami}}, {{jalink|Mizuhanome|Q8191796|Mizuhanome no Kami}} Kawashima-kawasukune-jinja Main hall.jpg|Main hall Kawashima-kawasukune-jinja Main hall inside.jpg|Main hall decorations === Subordinate Shrines === '''Sessha''' * Suwa Shrine : (Primary deities) {{jalink|Takeminakata|Q9150391|Takeminakata no Kami}}, {{jalink|Yasakatome|Q9150392|Yasakatome no Kami}} :: Due to mudflows from the eruption of Mount Asama in Tenmei 3, it was relocated together with Kawasukune Shrine from Aza Butai to the current site. The “Kawashima Lion Dance” is a ritual of Suwa Shrine{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=875-876}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|pp=639-643}}. * Shinmei Shrine : (Primary deity) {{jalink|Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|Amaterasu Ōmikami}} :: The former “Ise Shrine” enshrined at Tenjinhara was relocated and renamed Shinmei Shrine{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=876-877}}. * Inari Shrine – (Primary deity) {{jalink|Ukanomitama|Q3080728|Ukanomitama no Kami}} * Hokushin Shrine – (Primary deity) Inarisha.jpg|Inari Shrine Kawasukune-jinja(Kawajima) Shinmyousha.jpg|Shinmei Shrine Kawasukune-jinja(Kawajima,Shibukawa) Hokushinsha.jpg|Hokushin Shrine === Cultural Properties === === Shibukawa City Designated Important Tangible Cultural Property === * Kawasukune Shrine {{jalink|Sangaku|Q2569069}} (designated January 30, Showa 50){{Cite web |title=Designated Cultural Properties of Former Shibukawa City Area |website=www.city.shibukawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-08-20 |language=ja}} – Measuring 190 cm in width and 80 cm in height, it was dedicated in {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}} 3 ({{jalink|1856|Q7676}}) by Chiaki Keietsu, a native of Tsukiji Village, {{jalink|Tone District|Q1077001}} (present-day {{jalink|Katashina Village|Q1203661}}). The sangaku calculates the base length of a right triangle using algebra{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1986|pp=273-274}}. === Shibukawa City Designated Important Intangible Cultural Property === * Kawashima {{jalink|Lion Dance|Q713866}} (designated October 20, Showa 47) – A ritual performed by the Kawashima Lion Dance Preservation Society to pray for warding off evil, good health, and abundant harvests. It is dedicated at the annual festival of the subordinate Suwa Shrine (early October). Though believed to have been interrupted by the eruption of Mount Asama in Tenmei 3 (1783), an extant storage box for the lion heads bears the date {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} 4 ({{jalink|1833|Q7587}}), suggesting it was revived around that time. The dance is performed by a single dancer wearing a waist drum, and the lion heads consist of three types: male lion (Hōgan), female lion, and child lion{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1986|pp=299-230}}. === Access === * 15-minute walk from {{jalink|Kanashima Station (Gunma Prefecture)|Q6360686|Kanashima Station}} on the {{jalink|Agatsuma Line|Q391568}} of {{jalink|East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|JR East}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book|ja |title=Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Gazetteer |date=1925-10-20 |year=1925 |publisher=Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Education Association |ref=harv |author=Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Education Association |doi=10.11501/1020918}} * {{Cite book|ja |title=Stone Monuments and Cultural Properties |date=1986-3-31 |year=1986 |publisher=Shibukawa City |ref=harv |editor=Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee |doi=10.11501/12424075}}{{Registration required}} * {{Cite book|ja |title=History of Shibukawa City |date=1993-3-31 |year=1993 |publisher=Shibukawa City |ref=harv |editor=Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee |volume=Vol. 2: General History (Ancient–Early Modern) |doi=10.11501/9644681}}{{Registration required}} * {{Cite book|ja |title=History of Shibukawa City |date=1984-10-1 |year=1984 |publisher=Shibukawa City |ref=harv |editor=Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee |volume=Vol. 4: Folklore |doi=10.11501/9643284}}{{Registration required}} === Related Articles === * {{jalink|List of Shikinaisha in Kōzuke Province|Q11360308}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Kawasukune Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kawasukune Shrine (Shibukawa)]][[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Kōzuke Province]] [[Category:群馬県の神社]] [[Category:群馬県の旧郷社]] [[Category:上野国の式内社]] [[Category:渋川市の建築物]] [[Category:渋川市の歴史]] [[Category:群馬県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:区市町村指定無形民俗文化財]] [[Category:pages]]

kune Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kawasukune Shrine''' (かわすくねじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Kawashima, {{jalink|Shibukawa City|Q851492}}, {{jalink|Gunma Prefecture|Q129499}}. The enshrined deities are {{jalink|Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}} and {{jalink|Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|Hayakitsuhime no Kami}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|p=543}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha||minor shrine listed in the Engishiki}} of {{jalink|Kōzuke Province|Q1054838}}, {{jalink|Gunma District|Q5617131}}. Its former shrine rank was {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}. === History === It is said to have been founded on September 29, {{jalink|Hōki|Q18520676}} 2 ({{jalink|771|Q29950}}){{Sfn|Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Education Association|1925|pp=575-577}}. According to the shrine records, the Kawasukune Shrine in Hakoshima Village (present-day {{jalink|Higashiagatsuma Town|Q1203419}} Hakoshima), the Sukune Shrine in Ubajima Village (present-day Ubajima, Shibukawa City; now {{jalink|Takeuchi Shrine (Shibukawa City)|Q66085129|Takeuchi Shrine}}), and the Kawasukune Shrine in Yuage Village (present-day Miyukida, Shibukawa City) were all divided and enshrined from this shrine during the {{jalink|Bunan|Q1193586}} era ({{jalink|1444|Q6420}}–{{jalink|1449|Q6446}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=883-884,933-934}}. Interpreting “Kōha” as “river” and “Sukune” as “straight root,” the shrine name is understood to mean “the main stream of the river.” Because three shrines stood along the {{jalink|Agatsuma River|Q1636255}} in places named Hakoshima, Ubajima, and Kawashima—all containing the word “island”—{{jalink|Kizao Ozaki|Q21698487}} explains that the river itself was deified{{Cite book|ja |title=Research on the Jinmyōchō of Kōzuke Province |date=1974-12-10 |year=1974 |publisher=Ozaki-sensei Book Publication Society |pages=210-212 |last=Ozaki |first=Kizao |author-link=Kizao Ozaki |doi=10.11501/12266236}}{{Registration required}}. In July of {{jalink|Tenmei|Q1194121}} 3 ({{jalink|1783|Q7745}}), the shrine buildings were washed away by mudflows caused by the {{jalink|Tenmei Great Eruption|Q106959236|eruption of Mount Asama}}, but they were rebuilt at the present location on September 19, Tenmei 5 ({{jalink|1785|Q7756}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=235}}. The main hall dates to the Tenmei era, and the worship hall is believed—based on a {{jalink|munafuda|Q8008834}}—to have been built in {{jalink|Kōka|Q1325317}} 2 ({{jalink|1845|Q7636}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=236}}. During the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, a Shugendō temple called Kōmyō-ji existed as the shrine’s {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755}}, but it was abolished after the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=237,960-962}}. The “Kawashima Lion Dance” is a ritual of Suwa Shrine{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=875-876}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|pp=639-643}}. * Shinmei Designated Important Tangible Cultural Property === * Kawasukune Shrine {{jalink|Sangaku|Q2569069}} (designated January 30, Showa 50){{Cite web |title=Designated Cultural Properties of Former Shibukawa City Area |website=www.city.shibukawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-08-20 |language=ja}} – Measuring 190 cm in width and 80 cm in height, it was dedicated in {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}} 3 ({{jalink|1856|Q7676}}) by Chiaki Keietsu, a native of Tsukiji Village, {{jalink|Tone District|Q1077001}} (present-day {{jalink|Katashina Village|Q1203661}}). The sangaku calculates the base length of a right triangle using algebra{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1986|pp=273-274}}. === Shibukawa City Designated Important Intangible Cultural Property === * Kawashima {{jalink|Lion Dance|Q713866}} (designated October 20, Showa 47) – A ritual performed by the Kawashima Lion Dance Preservation Society to pray for warding off evil, good health, and abundant harvests. It is dedicated at the annual festival of the subordinate Suwa Shrine (early October). Though believed to have been interrupted by the eruption of Mount Asama in Tenmei 3 (1783), an extant storage box for the lion heads bears the date {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} 4 ({{jalink|1833|Q7587}}), suggesting it was revived around that time. The dance is performed by a single dancer wearing a waist drum, and the lion heads consist of three types: male lion (Hōgan), female lion, and child lion{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1986|pp=299-230}}. === Access === * 15-minute walk from {{jalink|Kanashima Station (Gunma Prefecture)|Q6360686|Kanashima Station}} on the {{jalink|Agatsuma Line|Q391568}} of {{jalink|East Worship hall |Location = {{jalink|Gunma Prefecture|Q129499}}{{jalink|Shibukawa City|Q851492}} Kawashima 1287 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deities = {{Ublist| {{jalink|Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}} | {{jalink|Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|Hayakitsuhime no Kami}} }} |Sacred Object = {{jalink|Agatsuma River|Q1636255}} |Shrine Rank = {{Ublist| {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} (minor) candidate shrine | {{jalink|Kōzuke Province|Q1054838}}{{jalink|Ichinomiya|Q1656379|Fourth Shrine}} | Former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} }} |Founded = {{jalink|Hōki|Q18520676}} 2 ({{jalink|771|Q29950}}) September 29 |Chief Priest = |Main Hall Style = {{jalink|Nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Other Name = Sukune Daimyōjin |Pilgrimage Status = |Annual Festival = Sunday closest to {{jalink|October 9|Q2923}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Gunma|label=Kawasu
kune Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kawasukune Shrine''' (かわすくねじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Kawashima, {{jalink|Shibukawa City|Q851492}}, {{jalink|Gunma Prefecture|Q129499}}. The enshrined deities are {{jalink|Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}} and {{jalink|Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|Hayakitsuhime no Kami}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|p=543}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha||minor shrine listed in the Engishiki}} of {{jalink|Kōzuke Province|Q1054838}}, {{jalink|Gunma District|Q5617131}}. Its former shrine rank was {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}}. === History === It is said to have been founded on September 29, {{jalink|Hōki|Q18520676}} 2 ({{jalink|771|Q29950}}){{Sfn|Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Education Association|1925|pp=575-577}}. According to the shrine records, the Kawasukune Shrine in Hakoshima Village (present-day {{jalink|Higashiagatsuma Town|Q1203419}} Hakoshima), the Sukune Shrine in Ubajima Village (present-day Ubajima, Shibukawa City; now {{jalink|Takeuchi Shrine (Shibukawa City)|Q66085129|Takeuchi Shrine}}), and the Kawasukune Shrine in Yuage Village (present-day Miyukida, Shibukawa City) were all divided and enshrined from this shrine during the {{jalink|Bunan|Q1193586}} era ({{jalink|1444|Q6420}}–{{jalink|1449|Q6446}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=883-884,933-934}}. Interpreting “Kōha” as “river” and “Sukune” as “straight root,” the shrine name is understood to mean “the main stream of the river.” Because three shrines stood along the {{jalink|Agatsuma River|Q1636255}} in places named Hakoshima, Ubajima, and Kawashima—all containing the word “island”—{{jalink|Kizao Ozaki|Q21698487}} explains that the river itself was deified{{Cite book|ja |title=Research on the Jinmyōchō of Kōzuke Province |date=1974-12-10 |year=1974 |publisher=Ozaki-sensei Book Publication Society |pages=210-212 |last=Ozaki |first=Kizao |author-link=Kizao Ozaki |doi=10.11501/12266236}}{{Registration required}}. In July of {{jalink|Tenmei|Q1194121}} 3 ({{jalink|1783|Q7745}}), the shrine buildings were washed away by mudflows caused by the {{jalink|Tenmei Great Eruption|Q106959236|eruption of Mount Asama}}, but they were rebuilt at the present location on September 19, Tenmei 5 ({{jalink|1785|Q7756}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=235}}. The main hall dates to the Tenmei era, and the worship hall is believed—based on a {{jalink|munafuda|Q8008834}}—to have been built in {{jalink|Kōka|Q1325317}} 2 ({{jalink|1845|Q7636}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=236}}. During the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}, a Shugendō temple called Kōmyō-ji existed as the shrine’s {{jalink|bettō-ji|Q11396755}}, but it was abolished after the {{jalink|Meiji Restoration|Q8707}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=237,960-962}}. Inarisha.jpg|Inari Shrine Kawasukune-jinja(Kawajima) Shinmyousha.jpg|Shinmei Shrine Kawasukune-jinja(Kawajima,Shibukawa) Hokushinsha.jpg|Hokushin Shrine === Cultural Properties === === Shibukawa City Designated Important Tangible Cultural Property === * Kawasukune Shrine {{jalink|Sangaku|Q2569069}} (designated January 30, Showa 50){{Cite web |title=Designated Cultural Properties of Former Shibukawa City Area |website=www.city.shibukawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-08-20 |language=ja}} – Measuring 190 cm in width and 80 cm in height, it was dedicated in {{jalink|Ansei|Q1327259}} 3 ({{jalink|1856|Q7676}}) by Chiaki Keietsu, a native of Tsukiji Village, {{jalink|Tone District|Q1077001}} (present-day {{jalink|Katashina Village|Q1203661}}). The sangaku calculates the base length of a right triangle using algebra{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1986|pp=273-274}}. === Shibukawa City Designated Important Intangible Cultural Property === * Kawashima {{jalink|Lion Dance|Q713866}} (designated October 20, Showa 47) – A ritual performed by the Kawashima Lion Dance Preservation Society to pray for warding off evil, good health, and abundant harvests. It is dedicated at the annual festival of the subordinate Suwa Shrine (early October). Though believed to have been interrupted by the eruption of Mount Asama in Tenmei 3 (1783), an extant storage box for the lion heads bears the date {{jalink|Tenpō|Q1369045}} 4 ({{jalink|1833|Q7587}}), suggesting it was revived around that time. The dance is performed by a single dancer wearing a waist drum, and the lion heads consist of three types: male lion (Hōgan), female lion, and child lion{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1986|pp=299-230}}. === Access === * 15-minute walk from {{jalink|Kanashima Station (Gunma Prefecture)|Q6360686|Kanashima Station}} on the {{jalink|Agatsuma Line|Q391568}} of {{jalink|East Worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Gunma Prefecture|Q129499|WD=Q129499}}{{ill|lt=Shibukawa City|Q851492|WD=Q851492}} Kawashima 1287 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deities = {{Ublist| {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}} | {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhime no Kami}} }} |Sacred Object = {{ill|lt=Agatsuma River|Q1636255|WD=Q1636255}} |Shrine Rank = {{Ublist| {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (minor) candidate shrine | {{ill|lt=Kōzuke Province|Q1054838|WD=Q1054838}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Fourth Shrine}} | Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} }} |Founded = {{ill|lt=Hōki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}} 2 ({{ill|lt=771|Q29950|WD=Q29950}}) September 29 |Chief Priest = |Main Hall Style = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Other Name = Sukune Daimyōjin |Pilgrimage Status = |Annual Festival = Sunday closest to {{ill|lt=October 9|Q2923|WD=Q2923}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Gunma|label=Kawasu
kune Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kawasukune Shrine''' (かわすくねじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Kawashima, {{ill|lt=Shibukawa City|Q851492|WD=Q851492}}, {{ill|lt=Gunma Prefecture|Q129499|WD=Q129499}}. The enshrined deities are {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}} and {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhime no Kami}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|p=543}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha||WD=|lt=minor shrine listed in the Engishiki}} of {{ill|lt=Kōzuke Province|Q1054838|WD=Q1054838}}, {{ill|lt=Gunma District|Q5617131|WD=Q5617131}}. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === History === It is said to have been founded on September 29, {{ill|lt=Hōki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}} 2 ({{ill|lt=771|Q29950|WD=Q29950}}){{Sfn|Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Education Association|1925|pp=575-577}}. According to the shrine records, the Kawasukune Shrine in Hakoshima Village (present-day {{ill|lt=Higashiagatsuma Town|Q1203419|WD=Q1203419}} Hakoshima), the Sukune Shrine in Ubajima Village (present-day Ubajima, Shibukawa City; now {{ill|lt=Takeuchi Shrine (Shibukawa City)|Q66085129|WD=Q66085129|lt=Takeuchi Shrine}}), and the Kawasukune Shrine in Yuage Village (present-day Miyukida, Shibukawa City) were all divided and enshrined from this shrine during the {{ill|lt=Bunan|Q1193586|WD=Q1193586}} era ({{ill|lt=1444|Q6420|WD=Q6420}}–{{ill|lt=1449|Q6446|WD=Q6446}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=883-884,933-934}}. Interpreting “Kōha” as “river” and “Sukune” as “straight root,” the shrine name is understood to mean “the main stream of the river.” Because three shrines stood along the {{ill|lt=Agatsuma River|Q1636255|WD=Q1636255}} in places named Hakoshima, Ubajima, and Kawashima—all containing the word “island”—{{ill|lt=Kizao Ozaki|Q21698487|WD=Q21698487}} explains that the river itself was deified{{Cite book|ja |title=Research on the Jinmyōchō of Kōzuke Province |date=1974-12-10 |year=1974 |publisher=Ozaki-sensei Book Publication Society |pages=210-212 |last=Ozaki |first=Kizao |author-link=Kizao Ozaki |doi=10.11501/12266236}}{{Registration required}}. In July of {{ill|lt=Tenmei|Q1194121|WD=Q1194121}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1783|Q7745|WD=Q7745}}), the shrine buildings were washed away by mudflows caused by the {{ill|lt=Tenmei Great Eruption|Q106959236|WD=Q106959236|lt=eruption of Mount Asama}}, but they were rebuilt at the present location on September 19, Tenmei 5 ({{ill|lt=1785|Q7756|WD=Q7756}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=235}}. The main hall dates to the Tenmei era, and the worship hall is believed—based on a {{ill|lt=munafuda|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}}—to have been built in {{ill|lt=Kōka|Q1325317|WD=Q1325317}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1845|Q7636|WD=Q7636}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=236}}. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, a Shugendō temple called Kōmyō-ji existed as the shrine’s {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}}, but it was abolished after the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=237,960-962}}. {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhime no Kami}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City Village. Although a “Shrine Preservation Petition” submitted by parishioners in Meiji 11 was approved and spared them from consolidation, they were eventually consolidated in July Meiji 40{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=876}}. '''Additionally Enshrined''' * {{ill|lt=Ōmononushi|Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome no Kami}} Kawashima-kawasukune-jinja Main hall.jpg|Main hall Kawashima-kawasukune-jinja Main hall inside.jpg|Main hall decorations === Subordinate Shrines === '''Sessha''' * Suwa Shrine : (Primary deities) {{ill|lt=Takeminakata|Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Yasakatome|Q9150392|WD=Q9150392|lt=Yasakatome no Kami}} :: Due to mudflows from the eruption of Mount Asama in Tenmei 3, it was relocated together with Kawasukune Shrine from Aza Butai to the current site. The “Kawashima Lion Dance” is a ritual of Suwa Shrine{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=875-876}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|pp=639-643}}. * Shinmei Kawasukune-jinja(Kawajima,Shibukawa) Hokushinsha.jpg|Hokushin Shrine === Cultural Properties === === Shibukawa City Designated Important Tangible Cultural Property === * Kawasukune Shrine {{ill|lt=Sangaku|Q2569069|WD=Q2569069}} (designated January 30, Showa 50){{Cite web |title=Designated Cultural Properties of Former Shibukawa City Area |website=www.city.shibukawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-08-20 |language=ja}} – Measuring 190 cm in width and 80 cm in height, it was dedicated in {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1856|Q7676|WD=Q7676}}) by Chiaki Keietsu, a native of Tsukiji Village, {{ill|lt=Tone District|Q1077001|WD=Q1077001}} (present-day {{ill|lt=Katashina Village|Q1203661|WD=Q1203661}}). The sangaku calculates the base length of a right triangle using algebra{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1986|pp=273-274}}. === Shibukawa City Designated Important Intangible Cultural Property === * Kawashima {{ill|lt=Lion Dance|Q713866|WD=Q713866}} (designated October 20, Showa 47) – A ritual performed by the Kawashima Lion Dance Preservation Society to pray for warding off evil, good health, and abundant harvests. It is dedicated at the annual festival of the subordinate Suwa Shrine (early October). Though believed to have been interrupted by the eruption of Mount Asama in Tenmei 3 (1783), an extant storage box for the lion heads bears the date {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1833|Q7587|WD=Q7587}}), suggesting it was revived around that time. The dance is performed by a single dancer wearing a waist drum, and the lion heads consist of three types: male lion (Hōgan), female lion, and child lion{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1986|pp=299-230}}. === Access === * 15-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Kanashima Station (Gunma Prefecture)|Q6360686|WD=Q6360686|lt=Kanashima Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Agatsuma Line|Q391568|WD=Q391568}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book|ja |title=Gunma Worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Gunma Prefecture|Q129499|WD=Q129499}}{{ill|lt=Shibukawa City|Q851492|WD=Q851492}} Kawashima 1287 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deities = {{Ublist| {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}} | {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhime no Kami}} }} |Sacred Object = {{ill|lt=Agatsuma River|Q1636255|WD=Q1636255}} |Shrine Rank = {{Ublist| {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} (minor) candidate shrine | {{ill|lt=Kōzuke Province|Q1054838|WD=Q1054838}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Fourth Shrine}} | Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} }} |Founded = {{ill|lt=Hōki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}} 2 ({{ill|lt=771|Q29950|WD=Q29950}}) September 29 |Chief Priest = |Main Hall Style = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Other Name = Sukune Daimyōjin |Pilgrimage Status = |Annual Festival = Sunday closest to {{ill|lt=October 9|Q2923|WD=Q2923}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Gunma|label=Kawasu
kune Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kawasukune Shrine''' (かわすくねじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Kawashima, {{ill|lt=Shibukawa City|Q851492|WD=Q851492}}, {{ill|lt=Gunma Prefecture|Q129499|WD=Q129499}}. The enshrined deities are {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}} and {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhime no Kami}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|p=543}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha||WD=|lt=minor shrine listed in the Engishiki}} of {{ill|lt=Kōzuke Province|Q1054838|WD=Q1054838}}, {{ill|lt=Gunma District|Q5617131|WD=Q5617131}}. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === History === It is said to have been founded on September 29, {{ill|lt=Hōki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}} 2 ({{ill|lt=771|Q29950|WD=Q29950}}){{Sfn|Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Education Association|1925|pp=575-577}}. According to the shrine records, the Kawasukune Shrine in Hakoshima Village (present-day {{ill|lt=Higashiagatsuma Town|Q1203419|WD=Q1203419}} Hakoshima), the Sukune Shrine in Ubajima Village (present-day Ubajima, Shibukawa City; now {{ill|lt=Takeuchi Shrine (Shibukawa City)|Q66085129|WD=Q66085129|lt=Takeuchi Shrine}}), and the Kawasukune Shrine in Yuage Village (present-day Miyukida, Shibukawa City) were all divided and enshrined from this shrine during the {{ill|lt=Bunan|Q1193586|WD=Q1193586}} era ({{ill|lt=1444|Q6420|WD=Q6420}}–{{ill|lt=1449|Q6446|WD=Q6446}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=883-884,933-934}}. Interpreting “Kōha” as “river” and “Sukune” as “straight root,” the shrine name is understood to mean “the main stream of the river.” Because three shrines stood along the {{ill|lt=Agatsuma River|Q1636255|WD=Q1636255}} in places named Hakoshima, Ubajima, and Kawashima—all containing the word “island”—{{ill|lt=Kizao Ozaki|Q21698487|WD=Q21698487}} explains that the river itself was deified{{Cite book|ja |title=Research on the Jinmyōchō of Kōzuke Province |date=1974-12-10 |year=1974 |publisher=Ozaki-sensei Book Publication Society |pages=210-212 |last=Ozaki |first=Kizao |author-link=Kizao Ozaki |doi=10.11501/12266236}}{{Registration required}}. In July of {{ill|lt=Tenmei|Q1194121|WD=Q1194121}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1783|Q7745|WD=Q7745}}), the shrine buildings were washed away by mudflows caused by the {{ill|lt=Tenmei Great Eruption|Q106959236|WD=Q106959236|lt=eruption of Mount Asama}}, but they were rebuilt at the present location on September 19, Tenmei 5 ({{ill|lt=1785|Q7756|WD=Q7756}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=235}}. The main hall dates to the Tenmei era, and the worship hall is believed—based on a {{ill|lt=munafuda|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}}—to have been built in {{ill|lt=Kōka|Q1325317|WD=Q1325317}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1845|Q7636|WD=Q7636}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=236}}. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, a Shugendō temple called Kōmyō-ji existed as the shrine’s {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}}, but it was abolished after the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=237,960-962}}. {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhime no Kami}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|p=543}} '''Jointly Enshrined''' * Ōyamatsumi no Kami * Hondawake no Kami :: Deities formerly enshrined at the unranked Hachiman Shrine in Nishiozuka, Kawashima Village. Although a “Shrine Preservation Petition” submitted by parishioners in Meiji 11 was approved and spared them from consolidation, they were eventually consolidated in July Meiji 40{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=876}}. '''Additionally Enshrined''' * {{ill|lt=Ōmononushi|Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome no Kami}} Kawashima-kawasukune-jinja Main hall.jpg|Main hall Kawashima-kawasukune-jinja Main hall inside.jpg|Main hall decorations === Subordinate Shrines === '''Sessha''' * Suwa Shrine : (Primary deities) {{ill|lt=Takeminakata|Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Yasakatome|Q9150392|WD=Q9150392|lt=Yasakatome no Kami}} :: Due to mudflows from the eruption of Mount Asama in Tenmei 3, it was relocated together with Kawasukune Shrine from Aza Butai to the current site. The “Kawashima Lion Dance” is a ritual of Suwa Shrine{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=875-876}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|pp=639-643}}. * Shinmei Kawasukune-jinja(Kawajima,Shibukawa) Hokushinsha.jpg|Hokushin Shrine === Cultural Properties === === Shibukawa City Designated Important Tangible Cultural Property === * Kawasukune Shrine {{ill|lt=Sangaku|Q2569069|WD=Q2569069}} (designated January 30, Showa 50){{Cite web |title=Designated Cultural Properties of Former Shibukawa City Area |website=www.city.shibukawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-08-20 |language=ja}} – Measuring 190 cm in width and 80 cm in height, it was dedicated in {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1856|Q7676|WD=Q7676}}) by Chiaki Keietsu, a native of Tsukiji Village, {{ill|lt=Tone District|Q1077001|WD=Q1077001}} (present-day {{ill|lt=Katashina Village|Q1203661|WD=Q1203661}}). The sangaku calculates the base length of a right triangle using algebra{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1986|pp=273-274}}. === Shibukawa City Designated Important Intangible Cultural Property === * Kawashima {{ill|lt=Lion Dance|Q713866|WD=Q713866}} (designated October 20, Showa 47) – A ritual performed by the Kawashima Lion Dance Preservation Society to pray for warding off evil, good health, and abundant harvests. It is dedicated at the annual festival of the subordinate Suwa Shrine (early October). Though believed to have been interrupted by the eruption of Mount Asama in Tenmei 3 (1783), an extant storage box for the lion heads bears the date {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1833|Q7587|WD=Q7587}}), suggesting it was revived around that time. The dance is performed by a single dancer wearing a waist drum, and the lion heads consist of three types: male lion (Hōgan), female lion, and child lion{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1986|pp=299-230}}. === Access === * 15-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Kanashima Station (Gunma Prefecture)|Q6360686|WD=Q6360686|lt=Kanashima Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Agatsuma Line|Q391568|WD=Q391568}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book|ja |title=Gunma Worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Gunma Prefecture|Q129499|WD=Q129499}}{{ill|lt=Shibukawa City|Q851492|WD=Q851492}} Kawashima 1287 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deities = {{Ublist| {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}} | {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhime no Kami}} }} |Sacred Object = {{ill|lt=Agatsuma River|Q1636255|WD=Q1636255}} |Shrine Rank = {{Ublist| {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} (minor) candidate shrine | {{ill|lt=Kōzuke Province|Q1054838|WD=Q1054838}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Fourth Shrine}} | Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} }} |Founded = {{ill|lt=Hōki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}} 2 ({{ill|lt=771|Q29950|WD=Q29950}}) September 29 |Chief Priest = |Main Hall Style = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Other Name = Sukune Daimyōjin |Pilgrimage Status = |Annual Festival = Sunday closest to {{ill|lt=October 9|Q2923|WD=Q2923}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Gunma|label=Kawasu
kune Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kawasukune Shrine''' (かわすくねじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Kawashima, {{ill|lt=Shibukawa City|Q851492|WD=Q851492}}, {{ill|lt=Gunma Prefecture|Q129499|WD=Q129499}}. The enshrined deities are {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}} and {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhime no Kami}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|p=543}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha||WD=|lt=minor shrine listed in the Engishiki}} of {{ill|lt=Kōzuke Province|Q1054838|WD=Q1054838}}, {{ill|lt=Gunma District|Q5617131|WD=Q5617131}}. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === History === It is said to have been founded on September 29, {{ill|lt=Hōki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}} 2 ({{ill|lt=771|Q29950|WD=Q29950}}){{Sfn|Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Education Association|1925|pp=575-577}}. According to the shrine records, the Kawasukune Shrine in Hakoshima Village (present-day {{ill|lt=Higashiagatsuma Town|Q1203419|WD=Q1203419}} Hakoshima), the Sukune Shrine in Ubajima Village (present-day Ubajima, Shibukawa City; now {{ill|lt=Takeuchi Shrine (Shibukawa City)|Q66085129|WD=Q66085129|lt=Takeuchi Shrine}}), and the Kawasukune Shrine in Yuage Village (present-day Miyukida, Shibukawa City) were all divided and enshrined from this shrine during the {{ill|lt=Bunan|Q1193586|WD=Q1193586}} era ({{ill|lt=1444|Q6420|WD=Q6420}}–{{ill|lt=1449|Q6446|WD=Q6446}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=883-884,933-934}}. Interpreting “Kōha” as “river” and “Sukune” as “straight root,” the shrine name is understood to mean “the main stream of the river.” Because three shrines stood along the {{ill|lt=Agatsuma River|Q1636255|WD=Q1636255}} in places named Hakoshima, Ubajima, and Kawashima—all containing the word “island”—{{ill|lt=Kizao Ozaki|Q21698487|WD=Q21698487}} explains that the river itself was deified{{Cite book|ja |title=Research on the Jinmyōchō of Kōzuke Province |date=1974-12-10 |year=1974 |publisher=Ozaki-sensei Book Publication Society |pages=210-212 |last=Ozaki |first=Kizao |author-link=Kizao Ozaki |doi=10.11501/12266236}}{{Registration required}}. In July of {{ill|lt=Tenmei|Q1194121|WD=Q1194121}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1783|Q7745|WD=Q7745}}), the shrine buildings were washed away by mudflows caused by the {{ill|lt=Tenmei Great Eruption|Q106959236|WD=Q106959236|lt=eruption of Mount Asama}}, but they were rebuilt at the present location on September 19, Tenmei 5 ({{ill|lt=1785|Q7756|WD=Q7756}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=235}}. The main hall dates to the Tenmei era, and the worship hall is believed—based on a {{ill|lt=munafuda|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}}—to have been built in {{ill|lt=Kōka|Q1325317|WD=Q1325317}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1845|Q7636|WD=Q7636}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=236}}. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, a Shugendō temple called Kōmyō-ji existed as the shrine’s {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}}, but it was abolished after the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=237,960-962}}. Association|1925|pp=575-577}} * Meiji 13 (1881) May – GōshaMeiji Shrine Records{{Sfn|Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Education Association|1925|pp=575-577}} * Meiji 42 (1909) February 27 – Shrine receiving offerings of sacred food and ritual implements{{Sfn|Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Education Association|1925|pp=575-577}} === Enshrined Deities === '''Primary Deities''' * {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhime no Kami}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|p=543}} '''Jointly Enshrined''' * Ōyamatsumi no Kami * Hondawake no Kami :: Deities formerly enshrined at the unranked Hachiman Shrine in Nishiozuka, Kawashima Village. Although a “Shrine Preservation Petition” submitted by parishioners in Meiji 11 was approved and spared them from consolidation, they were eventually consolidated in July Meiji 40{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=876}}. '''Additionally Enshrined''' * {{ill|lt=Ōmononushi|Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome no Kami}} Kawashima-kawasukune-jinja Main hall.jpg|Main hall Kawashima-kawasukune-jinja Main hall inside.jpg|Main hall decorations === Subordinate Shrines === '''Sessha''' * Suwa Shrine : (Primary deities) {{ill|lt=Takeminakata|Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Yasakatome|Q9150392|WD=Q9150392|lt=Yasakatome no Kami}} :: Due to mudflows from the eruption of Mount Asama in Tenmei 3, it was relocated together with Kawasukune Shrine from Aza Butai to the current site. The “Kawashima Lion Dance” is a ritual of Suwa Shrine{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=875-876}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|pp=639-643}}. * Shinmei Shrine : (Primary deity) {{ill|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|Q455602|WD=Q455602|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami}} :: The former “Ise Shrine” enshrined at Tenjinhara was relocated and renamed Shinmei Shrine{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=876-877}}. * Inari Shrine – (Primary deity) {{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama no Kami}} * Hokushin Shrine – (Primary deity) {{ill|lt=Takamimusubi||WD=|lt=Takamimusubi no Kami}}, Amaterasu Ōmikami * Tenjin Shrine * {{ill|lt=Sarutahiko|Q3090037|WD=Q3090037|lt=Sarutahiko Ōkami}} * Kōshin Shrine * Kuraikami – consolidated on June 18, {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1796|Q7818|WD=Q7818}}) * Gozu Tennō – consolidated on June 15, {{ill|lt=Kansei|Q1195131|WD=Q1195131}} 7 ({{ill|lt=1796|Q7818|WD=Q7818}}) * {{ill|lt=Kenrō Jishin|Q11427926|WD=Q11427926}} – consolidated December {{ill|lt=Kyōwa|Q960763|WD=Q960763}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1804|Q6894|WD=Q6894}}) * {{ill|lt=Daikokuten|Q1759916|WD=Q1759916}} Kawasukune-jinja(Kawagima,Shibukawa) Suwa-jinja.jpg|Suwa Shrine Kawasukune-jinja(Kawajima,Shibukawa) Inarisha.jpg|Inari Shrine Kawasukune-jinja(Kawajima) Shinmyousha.jpg|Shinmei Shrine Kawasukune-jinja(Kawajima,Shibukawa) Hokushinsha.jpg|Hokushin Shrine === Cultural Properties === === Shibukawa City Designated Important Tangible Cultural Property === * Kawasukune Shrine {{ill|lt=Sangaku|Q2569069|WD=Q2569069}} (designated January 30, Showa 50){{Cite web |title=Designated Cultural Properties of Former Shibukawa City Area |website=www.city.shibukawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-08-20 |language=ja}} – Measuring 190 cm in width and 80 cm in height, it was dedicated in {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1856|Q7676|WD=Q7676}}) by Chiaki Keietsu, a native of Tsukiji Village, {{ill|lt=Tone District|Q1077001|WD=Q1077001}} (present-day {{ill|lt=Katashina Village|Q1203661|WD=Q1203661}}). The sangaku calculates the base length of a right triangle using algebra{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1986|pp=273-274}}. === Shibukawa City Designated Important Intangible Cultural Property === * Kawashima {{ill|lt=Lion Dance|Q713866|WD=Q713866}} (designated October 20, Showa 47) – A ritual performed by the Kawashima Lion Dance Preservation Society to pray for warding off evil, good health, and abundant harvests. It is dedicated at the annual festival of the subordinate Suwa Shrine (early October). Though believed to have been interrupted by the eruption of Mount Asama in Tenmei 3 (1783), an extant storage box for the lion heads bears the date {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1833|Q7587|WD=Q7587}}), suggesting it was revived around that time. The dance is performed by a single dancer wearing a waist drum, and the lion heads consist of three types: male lion (Hōgan), female lion, and child lion{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1986|pp=299-230}}. === Access === * 15-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Kanashima Station (Gunma Prefecture)|Q6360686|WD=Q6360686|lt=Kanashima Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Agatsuma Line|Q391568|WD=Q391568}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book|ja |title=Gunma |doi=10.11501/1020918}} * {{Cite book|ja |title=Stone Monuments and Cultural Properties |date=1986-3-31 |year=1986 |publisher=Shibukawa City |ref=harv |editor=Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee |doi=10.11501/12424075}}{{Registration required}} * {{Cite book|ja |title=History of Shibukawa City |date=1993-3-31 |year=1993 |publisher=Shibukawa City |ref=harv |editor=Shibukawa City kune Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kawasukune Shrine''' (かわすくねじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Kawashima, {{ill|lt=Shibukawa City|Q851492|WD=Q851492}}, {{ill|lt=Gunma Prefecture|Q129499|WD=Q129499}}. The enshrined deities are {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}} and {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhime no Kami}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|p=543}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN|WD=|lt=minor shrine listed in the Engishiki}} of {{ill|lt=Kōzuke Province|Q1054838|WD=Q1054838}}, {{ill|lt=Gunma District|Q5617131|WD=Q5617131}}. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === History === It is said to have been founded on September 29, {{ill|lt=Hōki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}} 2 ({{ill|lt=771|Q29950|WD=Q29950}}){{Sfn|Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Education Association|1925|pp=575-577}}. According to the shrine records, the Kawasukune Shrine in Hakoshima Village (present-day {{ill|lt=Higashiagatsuma Town|Q1203419|WD=Q1203419}} Hakoshima), the Sukune Shrine in Ubajima Village (present-day Ubajima, Shibukawa City; now {{ill|lt=Takeuchi Shrine (Shibukawa City)|Q66085129|WD=Q66085129|lt=Takeuchi Shrine}}), and the Kawasukune Shrine in Yuage Village (present-day Miyukida, Shibukawa City) were all divided and enshrined from this shrine during the {{ill|lt=Bunan|Q1193586|WD=Q1193586}} era ({{ill|lt=1444|Q6420|WD=Q6420}}–{{ill|lt=1449|Q6446|WD=Q6446}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=883-884,933-934}}. Interpreting “Kōha” as “river” and “Sukune” as “straight root,” the shrine name is understood to mean “the main stream of the river.” Because three shrines stood along the {{ill|lt=Agatsuma River|Q1636255|WD=Q1636255}} in places named Hakoshima, Ubajima, and Kawashima—all containing the word “island”—{{ill|lt=Kizao Ozaki|Q21698487|WD=Q21698487}} explains that the river itself was deified{{Cite book|ja |title=Research on the Jinmyōchō of Kōzuke Province |date=1974-12-10 |year=1974 |publisher=Ozaki-sensei Book Publication Society |pages=210-212 |last=Ozaki |first=Kizao |author-link=Kizao Ozaki |doi=10.11501/12266236}}{{Registration required}}. In July of {{ill|lt=Tenmei|Q1194121|WD=Q1194121}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1783|Q7745|WD=Q7745}}), the shrine buildings were washed away by mudflows caused by the {{ill|lt=Tenmei Great Eruption|Q106959236|WD=Q106959236|lt=eruption of Mount Asama}}, but they were rebuilt at the present location on September 19, Tenmei 5 ({{ill|lt=1785|Q7756|WD=Q7756}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=235}}. The main hall dates to the Tenmei era, and the worship hall is believed—based on a {{ill|lt=munafuda|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}}—to have been built in {{ill|lt=Kōka|Q1325317|WD=Q1325317}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1845|Q7636|WD=Q7636}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=236}}. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, a Shugendō temple called Kōmyō-ji existed as the shrine’s {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}}, but it was abolished after the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=237,960-962}}. {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhime no Kami}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|p=543}} '''Jointly Enshrined''' * Ōyamatsumi no Kami * Hondawake no Kami :: Deities formerly enshrined at the unranked Hachiman Shrine in Nishiozuka, Kawashima Village. Although a “Shrine Preservation Petition” submitted by parishioners in Meiji 11 was approved and spared them from consolidation, they were eventually consolidated in July Meiji 40{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=876}}. '''Additionally Enshrined''' * {{ill|lt=Ōmononushi|Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome no Kami}} Kawashima-kawasukune-jinja Main hall.jpg|Main hall Kawashima-kawasukune-jinja Main hall inside.jpg|Main hall decorations === Subordinate Shrines === '''Sessha''' * Suwa Shrine : (Primary deities) {{ill|lt=Takeminakata|Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Yasakatome|Q9150392|WD=Q9150392|lt=Yasakatome no Kami}} :: Due to mudflows from the eruption of Mount Asama in Tenmei 3, it was relocated together with Kawasukune Shrine from Aza Butai to the current site. The “Kawashima Lion Dance” is a ritual of Suwa Shrine{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=875-876}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|pp=639-643}}. * Shinmei Shinmyousha.jpg|Shinmei Shrine Kawasukune-jinja(Kawajima,Shibukawa) Hokushinsha.jpg|Hokushin Shrine === Cultural Properties === === Shibukawa City Designated Important Tangible Cultural Property === * Kawasukune Shrine {{ill|lt=Sangaku|Q2569069|WD=Q2569069}} (designated January 30, Showa 50){{Cite web |title=Designated Cultural Properties of Former Shibukawa City Area |website=www.city.shibukawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-08-20 |language=ja}} – Measuring 190 cm in width and 80 cm in height, it was dedicated in {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1856|Q7676|WD=Q7676}}) by Chiaki Keietsu, a native of Tsukiji Village, {{ill|lt=Tone District|Q1077001|WD=Q1077001}} (present-day {{ill|lt=Katashina Village|Q1203661|WD=Q1203661}}). The sangaku calculates the base length of a right triangle using algebra{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1986|pp=273-274}}. === Shibukawa City Designated Important Intangible Cultural Property === * Kawashima {{ill|lt=Lion Dance|Q713866|WD=Q713866}} (designated October 20, Showa 47) – A ritual performed by the Kawashima Lion Dance Preservation Society to pray for warding off evil, good health, and abundant harvests. It is dedicated at the annual festival of the subordinate Suwa Shrine (early October). Though believed to have been interrupted by the eruption of Mount Asama in Tenmei 3 (1783), an extant storage box for the lion heads bears the date {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1833|Q7587|WD=Q7587}}), suggesting it was revived around that time. The dance is performed by a single dancer wearing a waist drum, and the lion heads consist of three types: male lion (Hōgan), female lion, and child lion{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1986|pp=299-230}}. === Access === * 15-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Kanashima Station (Gunma Prefecture)|Q6360686|WD=Q6360686|lt=Kanashima Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Agatsuma Line|Q391568|WD=Q391568}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book|ja |title=Gunma Worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Gunma Prefecture|Q129499|WD=Q129499}}{{ill|lt=Shibukawa City|Q851492|WD=Q851492}} Kawashima 1287 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deities = {{Ublist| {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}} | {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhime no Kami}} }} |Sacred Object = {{ill|lt=Agatsuma River|Q1636255|WD=Q1636255}} |Shrine Rank = {{Ublist| {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN}} (minor) candidate shrine | {{ill|lt=Kōzuke Province|Q1054838|WD=Q1054838}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Fourth Shrine}} | Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} }} |Founded = {{ill|lt=Hōki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}} 2 ({{ill|lt=771|Q29950|WD=Q29950}}) September 29 |Chief Priest = |Main Hall Style = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Other Name = Sukune Daimyōjin |Pilgrimage Status = |Annual Festival = Sunday closest to {{ill|lt=October 9|Q2923|WD=Q2923}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Gunma|label=Kawasu
kune Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kawasukune Shrine''' (かわすくねじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Kawashima, {{ill|lt=Shibukawa City|Q851492|WD=Q851492}}, {{ill|lt=Gunma Prefecture|Q129499|WD=Q129499}}. The enshrined deities are {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}} and {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhime no Kami}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|p=543}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN|lt=minor shrine listed in the Engishiki}} of {{ill|lt=Kōzuke Province|Q1054838|WD=Q1054838}}, {{ill|lt=Gunma District|Q5617131|WD=Q5617131}}. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === History === It is said to have been founded on September 29, {{ill|lt=Hōki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}} 2 ({{ill|lt=771|Q29950|WD=Q29950}}){{Sfn|Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Education Association|1925|pp=575-577}}. According to the shrine records, the Kawasukune Shrine in Hakoshima Village (present-day {{ill|lt=Higashiagatsuma Town|Q1203419|WD=Q1203419}} Hakoshima), the Sukune Shrine in Ubajima Village (present-day Ubajima, Shibukawa City; now {{ill|lt=Takeuchi Shrine (Shibukawa City)|Q66085129|WD=Q66085129|lt=Takeuchi Shrine}}), and the Kawasukune Shrine in Yuage Village (present-day Miyukida, Shibukawa City) were all divided and enshrined from this shrine during the {{ill|lt=Bunan|Q1193586|WD=Q1193586}} era ({{ill|lt=1444|Q6420|WD=Q6420}}–{{ill|lt=1449|Q6446|WD=Q6446}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=883-884,933-934}}. Interpreting “Kōha” as “river” and “Sukune” as “straight root,” the shrine name is understood to mean “the main stream of the river.” Because three shrines stood along the {{ill|lt=Agatsuma River|Q1636255|WD=Q1636255}} in places named Hakoshima, Ubajima, and Kawashima—all containing the word “island”—{{ill|lt=Kizao Ozaki|Q21698487|WD=Q21698487}} explains that the river itself was deified{{Cite book|ja |title=Research on the Jinmyōchō of Kōzuke Province |date=1974-12-10 |year=1974 |publisher=Ozaki-sensei Book Publication Society |pages=210-212 |last=Ozaki |first=Kizao |author-link=Kizao Ozaki |doi=10.11501/12266236}}{{Registration required}}. In July of {{ill|lt=Tenmei|Q1194121|WD=Q1194121}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1783|Q7745|WD=Q7745}}), the shrine buildings were washed away by mudflows caused by the {{ill|lt=Tenmei Great Eruption|Q106959236|WD=Q106959236|lt=eruption of Mount Asama}}, but they were rebuilt at the present location on September 19, Tenmei 5 ({{ill|lt=1785|Q7756|WD=Q7756}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=235}}. The main hall dates to the Tenmei era, and the worship hall is believed—based on a {{ill|lt=munafuda|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}}—to have been built in {{ill|lt=Kōka|Q1325317|WD=Q1325317}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1845|Q7636|WD=Q7636}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=236}}. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, a Shugendō temple called Kōmyō-ji existed as the shrine’s {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}}, but it was abolished after the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=237,960-962}}. {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhime no Kami}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|p=543}} '''Jointly Enshrined''' * Ōyamatsumi no Kami * Hondawake no Kami :: Deities formerly enshrined at the unranked Hachiman Shrine in Nishiozuka, Kawashima Village. Although a “Shrine Preservation Petition” submitted by parishioners in Meiji 11 was approved and spared them from consolidation, they were eventually consolidated in July Meiji 40{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=876}}. '''Additionally Enshrined''' * {{ill|lt=Ōmononushi|Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome no Kami}} Kawashima-kawasukune-jinja Main hall.jpg|Main hall Kawashima-kawasukune-jinja Main hall inside.jpg|Main hall decorations === Subordinate Shrines === '''Sessha''' * Suwa Shrine : (Primary deities) {{ill|lt=Takeminakata|Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Yasakatome|Q9150392|WD=Q9150392|lt=Yasakatome no Kami}} :: Due to mudflows from the eruption of Mount Asama in Tenmei 3, it was relocated together with Kawasukune Shrine from Aza Butai to the current site. The “Kawashima Lion Dance” is a ritual of Suwa Shrine{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=875-876}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|pp=639-643}}. * Shinmei Kawasukune-jinja(Kawajima,Shibukawa) Hokushinsha.jpg|Hokushin Shrine === Cultural Properties === === Shibukawa City Designated Important Tangible Cultural Property === * Kawasukune Shrine {{ill|lt=Sangaku|Q2569069|WD=Q2569069}} (designated January 30, Showa 50){{Cite web |title=Designated Cultural Properties of Former Shibukawa City Area |website=www.city.shibukawa.lg.jp |access-date=2024-08-20 |language=ja}} – Measuring 190 cm in width and 80 cm in height, it was dedicated in {{ill|lt=Ansei|Q1327259|WD=Q1327259}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1856|Q7676|WD=Q7676}}) by Chiaki Keietsu, a native of Tsukiji Village, {{ill|lt=Tone District|Q1077001|WD=Q1077001}} (present-day {{ill|lt=Katashina Village|Q1203661|WD=Q1203661}}). The sangaku calculates the base length of a right triangle using algebra{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1986|pp=273-274}}. === Shibukawa City Designated Important Intangible Cultural Property === * Kawashima {{ill|lt=Lion Dance|Q713866|WD=Q713866}} (designated October 20, Showa 47) – A ritual performed by the Kawashima Lion Dance Preservation Society to pray for warding off evil, good health, and abundant harvests. It is dedicated at the annual festival of the subordinate Suwa Shrine (early October). Though believed to have been interrupted by the eruption of Mount Asama in Tenmei 3 (1783), an extant storage box for the lion heads bears the date {{ill|lt=Tenpō|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1833|Q7587|WD=Q7587}}), suggesting it was revived around that time. The dance is performed by a single dancer wearing a waist drum, and the lion heads consist of three types: male lion (Hōgan), female lion, and child lion{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1986|pp=299-230}}. === Access === * 15-minute walk from {{ill|lt=Kanashima Station (Gunma Prefecture)|Q6360686|WD=Q6360686|lt=Kanashima Station}} on the {{ill|lt=Agatsuma Line|Q391568|WD=Q391568}} of {{ill|lt=East Japan Railway Company|Q499071|WD=Q499071|lt=JR East}} === Notes === {{Footnote help}} {{Reflist|2}} === References === * {{Cite book|ja |title=Gunma Worship hall |Location = {{ill|lt=Gunma Prefecture|Q129499|WD=Q129499}}{{ill|lt=Shibukawa City|Q851492|WD=Q851492}} Kawashima 1287 |Coordinates = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deities = {{Ublist| {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}} | {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhime no Kami}} }} |Sacred Object = {{ill|lt=Agatsuma River|Q1636255|WD=Q1636255}} |Shrine Rank = {{Ublist| {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} (minor) candidate shrine | {{ill|lt=Kōzuke Province|Q1054838|WD=Q1054838}}{{ill|lt=Ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|lt=Fourth Shrine}} | Former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} }} |Founded = {{ill|lt=Hōki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}} 2 ({{ill|lt=771|Q29950|WD=Q29950}}) September 29 |Chief Priest = |Main Hall Style = {{ill|lt=Nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Other Name = Sukune Daimyōjin |Pilgrimage Status = |Annual Festival = Sunday closest to {{ill|lt=October 9|Q2923|WD=Q2923}} |Rituals = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Gunma|label=Kawasu
kune Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Kawasukune Shrine''' (かわすくねじんじゃ) is a Shinto shrine located in Kawashima, {{ill|lt=Shibukawa City|Q851492|WD=Q851492}}, {{ill|lt=Gunma Prefecture|Q129499|WD=Q129499}}. The enshrined deities are {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}} and {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhime no Kami}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|p=543}}. It is a {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN|lt=minor shrine listed in the Engishiki}} of {{ill|lt=Kōzuke Province|Q1054838|WD=Q1054838}}, {{ill|lt=Gunma District|Q5617131|WD=Q5617131}}. Its former shrine rank was {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}}. === History === It is said to have been founded on September 29, {{ill|lt=Hōki|Q18520676|WD=Q18520676}} 2 ({{ill|lt=771|Q29950|WD=Q29950}}){{Sfn|Gunma Prefecture Gunma District Education Association|1925|pp=575-577}}. According to the shrine records, the Kawasukune Shrine in Hakoshima Village (present-day {{ill|lt=Higashiagatsuma Town|Q1203419|WD=Q1203419}} Hakoshima), the Sukune Shrine in Ubajima Village (present-day Ubajima, Shibukawa City; now {{ill|lt=Takeuchi Shrine (Shibukawa City)|Q66085129|WD=Q66085129|lt=Takeuchi Shrine}}), and the Kawasukune Shrine in Yuage Village (present-day Miyukida, Shibukawa City) were all divided and enshrined from this shrine during the {{ill|lt=Bunan|Q1193586|WD=Q1193586}} era ({{ill|lt=1444|Q6420|WD=Q6420}}–{{ill|lt=1449|Q6446|WD=Q6446}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=883-884,933-934}}. Interpreting “Kōha” as “river” and “Sukune” as “straight root,” the shrine name is understood to mean “the main stream of the river.” Because three shrines stood along the {{ill|lt=Agatsuma River|Q1636255|WD=Q1636255}} in places named Hakoshima, Ubajima, and Kawashima—all containing the word “island”—{{ill|lt=Kizao Ozaki|Q21698487|WD=Q21698487}} explains that the river itself was deified{{Cite book|ja |title=Research on the Jinmyōchō of Kōzuke Province |date=1974-12-10 |year=1974 |publisher=Ozaki-sensei Book Publication Society |pages=210-212 |last=Ozaki |first=Kizao |author-link=Kizao Ozaki |doi=10.11501/12266236}}{{Registration required}}. In July of {{ill|lt=Tenmei|Q1194121|WD=Q1194121}} 3 ({{ill|lt=1783|Q7745|WD=Q7745}}), the shrine buildings were washed away by mudflows caused by the {{ill|lt=Tenmei Great Eruption|Q106959236|WD=Q106959236|lt=eruption of Mount Asama}}, but they were rebuilt at the present location on September 19, Tenmei 5 ({{ill|lt=1785|Q7756|WD=Q7756}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=235}}. The main hall dates to the Tenmei era, and the worship hall is believed—based on a {{ill|lt=munafuda|Q8008834|WD=Q8008834}}—to have been built in {{ill|lt=Kōka|Q1325317|WD=Q1325317}} 2 ({{ill|lt=1845|Q7636|WD=Q7636}}){{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=236}}. During the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}, a Shugendō temple called Kōmyō-ji existed as the shrine’s {{ill|lt=bettō-ji|Q11396755|WD=Q11396755}}, but it was abolished after the {{ill|lt=Meiji Restoration|Q8707|WD=Q8707}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=237,960-962}}. {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhiko no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Hayakitsuhiko-Hayakitsuhime|Q11326733|WD=Q11326733|lt=Hayakitsuhime no Kami}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|p=543}} '''Jointly Enshrined''' * Ōyamatsumi no Kami * Hondawake no Kami :: Deities formerly enshrined at the unranked Hachiman Shrine in Nishiozuka, Kawashima Village. Although a “Shrine Preservation Petition” submitted by parishioners in Meiji 11 was approved and spared them from consolidation, they were eventually consolidated in July Meiji 40{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|p=876}}. '''Additionally Enshrined''' * {{ill|lt=Ōmononushi|Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Ukanomitama|Q3080728|WD=Q3080728|lt=Ukanomitama no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Mizuhanome|Q8191796|WD=Q8191796|lt=Mizuhanome no Kami}} Kawashima-kawasukune-jinja Main hall.jpg|Main hall Kawashima-kawasukune-jinja Main hall inside.jpg|Main hall decorations === Subordinate Shrines === '''Sessha''' * Suwa Shrine : (Primary deities) {{ill|lt=Takeminakata|Q9150391|WD=Q9150391|lt=Takeminakata no Kami}}, {{ill|lt=Yasakatome|Q9150392|WD=Q9150392|lt=Yasakatome no Kami}} :: Due to mudflows from the eruption of Mount Asama in Tenmei 3, it was relocated together with Kawasukune Shrine from Aza Butai to the current site. The “Kawashima Lion Dance” is a ritual of Suwa Shrine{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1993|pp=875-876}}{{Sfn|Shibukawa City History Compilation Committee|1984|pp=639-643}}. * Shinmei
